BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CON.:."lISSION 
In the Matter of: 
) 
) 
Opinion requested by 
) 
Assemblyman Art Torres ) 
--------------------------) 
No. 75-163 
Feb. 4 f 
1976 
BY THE COI,jlHSSIO)I: 
~'7e have been asked tr.e follm'ling 
questions by AsserllblYDan Art Torres: 
Assemblyman Art Torres was married after the effective 
da te of the Political Reform Act. 
He and his \·,i fe nei ther 
invited nor accepted gifts from lobbyists, so the fol1o~ing 
questions pertain only to the reporting requirements imposed 
by Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act: 
(1) 
l-xe Hedding 
Assemblyman Torres' i/life 
property and, therefore, 
gifts addressed to and received by 
prior to the marriage her separate 
not reportable by Assemblyman Torres? 
\~j 
If \~edding girts are COIlu'Tiur:ity property f 
l.~ C:>iC: 
~:1'-.J...Lc.; va:uc report:able 
or only one-half of the vaL.1.e? 
:;:-':,--
for example, an Osterizer cost $49.38, is the full value re-
portable by Assemblyman Torres or is only one-half the valt'e 
of the gift attributable to him and, therefore, not reportable 
because the value attributed to hiD would not exceed the $25.00 
threshold for reporting gifts? 
(3) 
Many Assemblyrrcen contributed to buy Asse2lb1yrr:an 
Torres and his wife a set of china dishes. 
Each individual 
share was less than $25.00, but the total cost of the dishes 
was greater than $25.00. 
How should that gift be reported? 
COr:CL US ION 
(I) 
For purposes of income reporting under Government 
Code Sections 87200, et seq., wedding gifts should be considered 
the property of both spouses unless they are peculiarly adaptable 
to the personal use of one spouse or specifically and unequivocally 
intended exclusively for use by one spouse. 
Conse~uently, wedding 
gifts may be repo~table by Assemblyman Torres even though they 
were received by his wi 
prior to the wedding. 


No. 75-163 
Page T'l.vo 
FPPC OPINIONS 32 
(2) 
Each spouse has a present, existing and equal 
interest In wedding gifts which are the property of both 
spouses. 
Assemblyman Torres must disclose only those gifts 
in which his interest exceeds the $25.00 threshold for report-
ing gifts. 
This means that wedding gifts, the total value of 
which is $50.00 or more, will be reportable by the AssemLlyman. 
(3) 
\'Then a single gift worth $25.00 or more is given 
by many donors 
each of whom contributed less than $25.00, the 
recipient should report receipt of the gift but need not dis-
close the name of every donor. 
It is sufficient to describe in 
general terms those who gave the gift. 
If, however, any con-
tributor gave $25.00 or more, his or her name must be disclosed. 
In this case, because the gift \'las given to both Assehlblyman 
Torres and his 
\,vife I 
the J.\s semblyman need only report the name 
of each donor who contributed $50.00 or more. 
ANALYSIS 
(1) 
As an elected state officer, Assemblyman Torres 
must file periodic statements disclosing his investments, his 
interests in real property and his income. 
Government Code 
Section 87203. ~/ ~'Jhen reporting his income, he is required to 
disclose the receipt of gifts from the same source aggregating 
twenty-five dollars ($25.00) or more in value and to specify 
the name and address of the source, the value of the gift and 
the date on which it was received. 
Section 87207. 
Because he 
was married after the effective date of the Political Reform 
Act, Sections 81000, et seq., 
Assemblyman Torres has asked 
a number of questions idi th respect to his reporting obligations 
concerning wedding gifts. 
His first question asks whether 
wedding gifts received by his bride prior to the marriage are 
reportable by him.~/ 
1/ 
-All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 
2/ 
-The reporting requirements imposed by the Political 
Reform Act do not apply to gifts from the filer's spouse, child, 
parent, grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-in-law, 
brother-in-law, sister-in-law, aunt, uncle, first cousin or the 
spouse of any such person. 
Section 82030. 
The analysis contained 
in this opinion applies only to wedding gifts which are received 
from persons other than close relatives.

All propcrty of

marriage, and that acquirej after~ard3 by gift, 
bequest, devise, or desce:1t, ... is her s2;.)arate 
property. 
Civil Code Sectio~ Sl07. 
Civil Code Section 5108 contains a:: ana1ogoi!S provision '.-lith 
regard to separate property of the husband.~/ 
A literal interpretation of these constitutional and 
statutory provisions supports a conclusion that ~edding gifts 
received by the bride before the wedding are her separate prop-
erty. 
However, it could be argued that wedding gifts are given 
in anticipation of the ensuing marriage relationship and are 
intended for the benefit and use of both spouses, even lr re-
ceived and acknowledged by the bride. 
Therefore, they are not, 
in one sense I 
"property of the \-life, m'lned by h2r before D3rri2ge .... 
Even if we conclude that the wedding gifts are separate pr 
rty 
at the time of marriage, persuasive arguments could be made to 
support the conclusion that the property is converted from sepa-
rate property to comr:luni ty property ,'illen the marriage takes pIece 
or some time shortly thereafter. 
Mears v. Mears, 180 Cal.App.2d 
484 (1960). 
The cases in our own and other jurisdictions afford 
little guidance for determining this perplexing problen.':U 1dhen 
3/ 
See also Cal. Const. Art. I, Sec. 21 which provlces: 
4/ 
Property m·med before marriage or acquired during 
marriage by gift, will, or inheritance is separate 
property. 
See Annot.: 
Rights in \"edding presents as bet'i!een 
spouses. 
75 A.L.R.2d 1365 (1961). 
There is some authority in our jurisdiction for deter-
mining ownership on the basis of equitable 
rather than legal 
interests when acquisition of the property spanned the time that 
marriage occurre4. 
For example, wages of one spouse earned be-
fore but collected after marriage are separate property. 
Thomasset v. Thomasset, 122 Cal.App.2d 116 (1953). 
On the other 
hand, real property purchased before marriage becomes community 
property to the extent that mortgage payments and other maintenance 
expenses are paid from community funds after the marriage. 
Bare v. 
Bare, 256 Cal.App.2d 684 (1967). 


? .:::c;c Four 
c2i.llcd upon to d.eteri7i.i~le the 
oE 
gi::ts, a 
~~~ York court has observed 25 
follo~s: 
The authorities \·:hLC:~ I 
21r:, calle.c: u:?2n to 
exa~ine are few and e.lusive to put it ~ildly. 
Indeed I find that o~ly Esily Post pres~~es to 
speak with confidence in t~is regard. 
Etiquette, 
Emily Post, Kew and Enldrged Edition, Funk and 
Wagnalls Company, page 322. 
"I'Yedding presents -
1 • 
ere a_1.. 
bric1e 1 
a!"'~(~ arc, accorc i:: 
--~--~~-~~------~~-~ 
personal property. 
It may be that in "polite" society all \·;edding 
presents are sent to the bride .... 
The fact is however that not all wedding guests 
have been trained in the niceties of "polite" society 
and unfortunately many of them have never read Miss 
Post's book .... 
While no one will challenge Miss Post's eminence 
as an arbiter of good taste, I doubt that her pro-
nouncement on the law in this regard may be accorded 
the dignity of stare decisis. 
It would have been 
nice had she cited a competent judicial decision of 
such sweeping magnitude. 
Had she done so there would 
be no need for this opinion. 
Avnet v. Avnet, 124 N.Y.S.2d 517, 521. 
(!-lun. Ct. 1953) 
After making these observations, the court went on to hold that: 
-:- this is the age of "50-50" marriages. 
The 
tlme has come to say clearly that all wedding 
gifts vlhether from the bride IS" side" or from 
the groom's, excepting such items which are pecul-
iarly adaptable to the personal use of either 
spouse, and those gifts which are specifically 
and unequivocally "earmarked" as intended exclu-
sively for one or the other of the spouses, ... 
are the joint property of both parties to the 
marriage. 
This reasoning should apply as well 
to things of like use purchased with cash wed-
ding gifts not othen'lise "earmarked." 
Avnet v. Avnet, 124 N.Y.S.2d at 524.

we believe that the in~eres~s of disclos~re un~e~ t~e Political 
that set forth in Avn~t.

gift is 1·cc2i\1'ec~ bj' the: t'?:iC2 pric:c to t~2 r:-:2:l...·1~i2;C! 
· ... -2 ... -:\0.--::.::0' 
gifts benefit both spouses and should be subject to disolosure 
unless ~eculiarly adap~able to the person~l use of one 
s~ouse 
or specifically inten~ed for t~e use of one spouse. 
Accord-
ingly, Assemblyman Torres should disclose receipt of ~edding 
gifts according to the guidelines which ~2 set forth in this 
opinion. 
( 2 ) 
Assemblyman Torres has asked ho~ he sho:"11c1 
the value of \'ledding gifts given to him and his \Vife. 
;'.sse±l'.·-
man Torres must disclose "his income" including speclrlc infor-
mation about gifts received by him that aggregate $25.00 or 
more in value. 
Sections 87203, 87207. 
~hen the gift is the 
property of both spouses, we interpret the disclosure require-
ment for gifts aggregating $25.00 or more in value to apply to 
the officeholder's interest in the gift, not to the total 
value of the gift. 
For those wedding gifts which are the property of both 
Assemblyman Torres and his wife, the Assemblyman's interest is 
one-half the value of the gift. 
Accordingly, the Assemblyman 
does not have to disclose the gift unless the total value 
equals or exceeds $50.00, in which case his one-half interest 
would equal or exceed $25.00. 
In the example posite6 by 
Assemblyman Torres, if an Osterizer cost $49.38, the Assembly-
man's interest in this community property is $24.69. 
Since 
his interest is less than $25.00, the Osterizer is not a re-
portable gift. 
Although the concept of COffilTIUnity property is not usu-
ally applied to determine the extent of one's interest in 
individual household items, our conclusion finds support in 
cOID..'1luni ty property la,·j. 
Civil Code Section 5105 provides: 
The respective interests of the husband 
and wife in cornmuni ty property during continu-
ance of the marriage relation are present, 
existing and equal interests .... 
Assemblyman Torres, therefore, has a one-half interest in the 
communi ty property oi.'med by him and his \c.,rife. 
See People v. 
Lockett, 25 Cal.App.3d 433 (1972). 
Consequently, we conclude 


No. 75-163 
Page Six 
that the disclosure 
of gifts required by Section 87207 is 
disclosure of one-half the value of a gift which is received 
by both spouses.~/ 
(3) 
Assemblyman Torres has asked how to report re-
ceipt of a set of china which is worth more than $25.00, but 
which was given by a number of Gonors, each of whon contrib-
uted less ~han $25.00. 
The disclosure requirements of the Political Refor~ 
Act, Sections 87200, et seq. r 
are triggered by 
t~~ v~lue of 
the gift received rather than by ~hom the gift is cade. 
~ 
set of china is a single gift for purposes of the ~ct and must 
be disclosed because the gift benefits both spouses a~d has 
a value of $50.00 or more:~/ 
Assemblyman Torres has indicated that many Assemblymen 
contributed to the purchase of the china. 
In general, the 
recipient of a reportable gift must disclose the name of each 
donor. 
However, where, as in this case, the gift was made by 
a large number of people 
none of whom contributed $50.00 or 
more,';ve do not believe that AssemblYDan Torres need disclose 
the name of each donor. 
His report is sufficient if he merely 
indicates that the gift \·las made by "Tr,'lenty members of the 
Assembly" or a similar designation that describes the group of 
persons making the gift. 
On the other hand, if he knc~s or has 
reason to know that any donor's share of the gift was $50.00 
or more, Assemblyman Torres should disclose the name of that 
donor.V 
5/ 
-Assemblyman Torres may not knm·; the exact value of each 
gift. 
A reasonable estimate based on a good faith effort to 
ascertain the value of the gift is sufficient to comply with 
the disclosure requirements of Section 87207. 
See Opinion 
requested by Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC Opinions 153 (No. 75-094-B, 
Oct. 23, 1975). 
6/ 
-Assemblyman Torres indicated that the value of the gift 
exceeded $25.00. 
As indicated by our analysis in Part 2 of 
this opinion, the gift was received by Assemblyman 'I'orres and 
his wife and, therefore, must be disclosed only if it has a value 
of $50.00 or more. 
We assume that the value of the set of china 
exceeds $50.00. 
7/ 
Section 81004 requires a filer to use reasonable dili-
gence in the preparation of reports. 
AsseIT~lyman Torres will be 
deemed to have used reasonable diligence if he re~orts information 
of which he has knowledge or that is readily discoverable. 
He 
need not conduct an independent investigation to determine the 
amount donated by each contributor.

COr:CL US ION 
(I) 
For purposes of income reporting under Government 
Code Sections 87200, et seq., wedding gifts should be considered

sections.conclusion: (1) For purposes of income reporting under Government Code Sections 87200, et seq., wedding gifts should be considered the property of both spouses unless they are peculiarly adaptable to the personal use of one spouse or specifically and unequivocally intended exclusively for use by one spouse. Consequently, wedding gifts may be reportable by Assemblyman Torres even though they were received by his wife prior to the wedding. (2) Each spouse has a present, existing and equal interest in wedding gifts which are the property of both spouses. Assemblyman Torres must disclose only those gifts in which his interest exceeds the $25.00 threshold for reporting gifts. This means that wedding gifts, the total value of which is $50.00 or more, will be reportable by the Assemblyman. (3) When a single gift worth $25.00 or more is given by many donors each of whom contributed less than $25.00, the recipient should report receipt of the gift but need not disclose the name of every donor. It is sufficient to describe in general terms those who gave the gift. If, however, any contributor gave $25.00 or more, his or her name must be disclosed. In this case, because the gift was given to both Assemblyman Torres and his wife, the Assemblyman need only report the name of each donor who contributed $50.00 or more.

set of china is a single gift for purposes of the ~ct and must 
be disclosed because the gift benefits both spouses a~d has 
a value of $50.00 or more:~/ 
Assemblyman Torres has indicated that many Assemblymen 
contributed to the purchase of the china. 
In general, the 
recipient of a reportable gift must disclose the name of each 
donor. 
However, where, as in this case, the gift was made by 
a large number of people 
none of whom contributed $50.00 or 
more,';ve do not believe that AssemblYDan Torres need disclose 
the name of each donor. 
His report is sufficient if he merely 
indicates that the gift \·las made by "Tr,'lenty members of the 
Assembly" or a similar designation that describes the group of 
persons making the gift. 
On the other hand, if he knc~s or has 
reason to know that any donor's share of the gift was $50.00 
or more, Assemblyman Torres should disclose the name of that 
donor.V 
5/ 
-Assemblyman Torres may not knm·; the exact value of each 
gift. 
A reasonable estimate based on a good faith effort to 
ascertain the value of the gift is sufficient to comply with 
the disclosure requirements of Section 87207. 
See Opinion 
requested by Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC Opinions 153 (No. 75-094-B, 
Oct. 23, 1975). 
6/ 
-Assemblyman Torres indicated that the value of the gift 
exceeded $25.00. 
As indicated by our analysis in Part 2 of 
this opinion, the gift was received by Assemblyman 'I'orres and 
his wife and, therefore, must be disclosed only if it has a value 
of $50.00 or more. 
We assume that the value of the set of china 
exceeds $50.00. 
7/ 
Section 81004 requires a filer to use reasonable dili-
gence in the preparation of reports. 
AsseIT~lyman Torres will be 
deemed to have used reasonable diligence if he re~orts information 
of which he has knowledge or that is readily discoverable. 
He 
need not conduct an independent investigation to determine the 
amount donated by each contributor.
sections.question_synthetic: Assemblyman Torres seeks clarification on reporting requirements for wedding gifts received before his marriage under the Political Reform Act, including whether gifts to his wife are reportable, how to value community property gifts, and how to report gifts given by multiple contributors.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that wedding gifts received before marriage are generally reportable as community property unless adapted for one spouse's sole use, that the Assemblyman need only report his one-half interest (requiring gifts totaling $50.00 or more to be reportable), and that group gifts need only identify donors contributing $50.00 or more rather than all contributors.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: High-quality OCR with clear question and conclusion sections. Document follows standard FPPC advice letter format with comprehensive analysis. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not affect core content extraction.

QUESTION: s by AsserllblYDan Art Torres: 
Assemblyman Art Torres was married after the effective 
da te of the Political Reform Act. 
He and his \·,i fe nei ther 
invited nor accepted gifts from lobbyists, so the fol1o~ing 
questions pertain only to the reporting requirements imposed 
by Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act: 
(1)

or only one-half of the vaL.1.e? 
:;:-':,--
for example, an Osterizer cost $49.38, is the full value re-
portable by Assemblyman Torres or is only one-half the valt'e 
of the gift attributable to him and, therefore, not reportable 
because the value attributed to hiD would not exceed the $25.00 
threshold for reporting gifts? 
(3) 
Many Assemblyrrcen contributed to buy Asse2lb1yrr:an 
Torres and his wife a set of china dishes. 
Each individual 
share was less than $25.00, but the total cost of the dishes 
was greater than $25.00. 
How should that gift be reported? 
COr:CL US ION 
(I) 
For purposes of income reporting under Government 
Code Sections 87200, et seq., wedding gifts should be considered 
the property of both spouses unless they are peculiarly adaptable 
to the personal use of one spouse or specifically and unequivocally 
intended exclusively for use by one spouse. 
Conse~uently, wedding 
gifts may be repo~table by Assemblyman Torres even though they 
were received by his wi 
prior to the wedding. 


No. 75-163 
Page T'l.vo 
FPPC OPINIONS 32 
(2) 
Each spouse has a present, existing and equal 
interest In wedding gifts which are the property of both 
spouses. 
Assemblyman Torres must disclose only those gifts 
in which his interest exceeds the $25.00 threshold for report-
ing gifts. 
This means that wedding gifts, the total value of 
which is $50.00 or more, will be reportable by the AssemLlyman. 
(3) 
\'Then a single gift worth $25.00 or more is given 
by many donors 
each of whom contributed less than $25.00, the 
recipient should report receipt of the gift but need not dis-
close the name of every donor. 
It is sufficient to describe in 
general terms those who gave the gift. 
If, however, any con-
tributor gave $25.00 or more, his or her name must be disclosed. 
In this case, because the gift \'las given to both Assehlblyman 
Torres and his 
\,vife I 
the J.\s semblyman need only report the name 
of each donor who contributed $50.00 or more.

CONCLUSION: (1) For purposes of income reporting under Government Code Sections 87200, et seq., wedding gifts should be considered the property of both spouses unless they are peculiarly adaptable to the personal use of one spouse or specifically and unequivocally intended exclusively for use by one spouse. Consequently, wedding gifts may be reportable by Assemblyman Torres even though they were received by his wife prior to the wedding. (2) Each spouse has a present, existing and equal interest in wedding gifts which are the property of both spouses. Assemblyman Torres must disclose only those gifts in which his interest exceeds the $25.00 threshold for reporting gifts. This means that wedding gifts, the total value of which is $50.00 or more, will be reportable by the Assemblyman. (3) When a single gift worth $25.00 or more is given by many donors each of whom contributed less than $25.00, the recipient should report receipt of the gift but need not disclose the name of every donor. It is sufficient to describe in general terms those who gave the gift. If, however, any contributor gave $25.00 or more, his or her name must be disclosed. In this case, because the gift was given to both Assemblyman Torres and his wife, the Assemblyman need only report the name of each donor who contributed $50.00 or more.

embedding.summary: FPPC Opinion 75-163 addresses Assemblyman Art Torres's three questions about reporting wedding gifts under the Political Reform Act, concluding that such gifts are generally community property requiring disclosure when his interest exceeds $25.00 (total value $50.00 or more) and establishing rules for reporting gifts from multiple contributors.
